text: {"matches":[{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":1,"rationale":"Claim 0 reports an observational association between coffee intake and lower depression rates, while Claim 1 asserts a causal preventive effect; they refer to the same exposure and outcome but differ in causal scope."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":2,"rationale":"Claim 0 describes an observational cohort result, whereas Claim 2 comments on the absence of a randomized trial; the latter concerns study design, not the exposure‑outcome relationship."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":2,"rationale":"Claim 1 makes a causal claim about coffee preventing depression, while Claim 2 states that no randomized trial was conducted; the statements address different aspects (causality vs evidence base)."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":3,"rationale":"Claim 0 links coffee consumption to depression rates, whereas Claim 3 links coffee consumption to higher exercise levels; they involve the same exposure but different outcomes."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":3,"rationale":"Claim 1 asserts coffee prevents depression, while Claim 3 notes that higher coffee consumers also exercise more; the claims concern different outcomes (depression vs exercise)."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":3,"rationale":"Claim 2 concerns the lack of a randomized trial, whereas Claim 3 describes an observational association between coffee intake and exercise; they address distinct aspects of the research."}]}
